title: Safety, Environment & IT Documentation
summary: How an embassy IT shop protects people and equipment, controls the physical and electrical environment, disposes of regulated materials safely, and governs work through asset management, documentation, and formal change control.
content:
Operational safety begins with protecting both people and hardware, and the most common hardware threat is electrostatic discharge (ESD). ESD is the sudden flow of static electricity between two objects at different electrical potentials — for example, a technician who has built up a charge by walking across carpet touching a memory module. The danger is that the threshold humans can *feel* (roughly 3,000 volts), *hear* (~5,000 V), or *see as a spark* (~10,000 V) is far above the threshold that *damages* components: as little as 10–30 volts can destroy a sensitive chip, so catastrophic or — worse — latent (delayed-failure) damage happens with no warning sign at all. Low humidity dramatically increases static buildup, which is why server rooms target roughly 40–60% relative humidity; too dry promotes ESD, too humid promotes condensation and corrosion.

ESD is controlled with a layered set of anti-static tools. An anti-static wrist strap connects the technician to a common ground through a built-in ~1 MΩ resistor that bleeds charge away slowly and safely; the resistor also protects the wearer from shock, which is exactly why you must NEVER wear an ESD strap while working on high-voltage devices such as a CRT monitor or a power supply. An anti-static (ESD) mat provides a grounded work surface, anti-static bags (the shiny metallic-gray Faraday type) shield stored components, and when no strap is available you self-ground by keeping continuous contact with a bare metal, grounded part of the chassis to equalize potential. Equipment grounding — bonding chassis to earth ground via the third (grounding) prong — and proper environmental humidity round out ESD defense.

• Electrical safety extends beyond ESD. Always power off and unplug equipment before servicing; treat power supplies (PSUs) and CRT monitors as dangerous even when unplugged because their capacitors store a lethal charge — these are not user-serviceable. A fuse is a sacrificial one-time device that melts to break a circuit on overcurrent; a circuit breaker performs the same protective function but can be reset. Know where the room's breaker panel and emergency power-off (EPO) are.
• Personal safety also covers physical hazards: lift with your legs, not your back, keep the load close to your body, avoid twisting, and get a second person or a cart for heavy gear (a common rule of thumb is to get help above ~50 lb / ~22 kg). Manage cables to prevent trip hazards and keep fire exits and extinguishers (use a Class C extinguisher for energized electrical fires) clear.

Environmental controls keep the data center healthy. HVAC maintains both temperature (server rooms commonly run ~18–27°C / 64–81°F per ASHRAE guidance) and humidity within range. Airflow is managed with a hot-aisle/cold-aisle layout so cold supply air and hot exhaust do not mix, and blanking panels fill empty rack units to prevent recirculation. Dust is an insulator and a thermal blanket — it clogs filters and fans and causes overheating — so filtered intake and regular cleaning (with compressed air and ESD-safe vacuums) matter. Positive air pressure helps keep dust out.

Power protection is a spectrum of increasing capability. A surge suppressor (surge protector) clamps transient voltage spikes only; its protection is rated in joules and it does nothing for sags, brownouts, or outages. An Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS) adds battery backup for ride-through and clean shutdown. UPS topologies differ: a standby (offline) UPS switches to battery on failure with a brief transfer time; a line-interactive UPS adds an automatic voltage regulator (AVR) to correct sags/surges without draining the battery, making it the common server-room choice; and an online (double-conversion) UPS continuously runs the load from the battery/inverter so there is zero transfer time and complete isolation — the best, most expensive option for critical loads. For sustained outages a standby generator (commonly diesel) provides long-run power; the UPS bridges the gap until the generator starts and stabilizes.

Safe handling and disposal of regulated materials is both a safety and a legal/compliance obligation. The authoritative reference for any chemical or material is its Safety Data Sheet (SDS, formerly MSDS), which lists hazards, handling, storage, PPE, first aid, and disposal. Specific hazards: lithium-ion batteries can catch fire or explode if punctured, shorted, or swollen and must be recycled, never thrown in regular trash; toner is a fine combustible powder (clean spills with a cold-water cloth and an ESD-safe/toner vacuum, not a standard vacuum that would spread it), and toner/ink cartridges should be returned/recycled; CRT monitors contain several pounds of lead and other heavy metals and are hazardous e-waste. All electronic waste should go to certified e-waste recycling, never landfill, and at an overseas post you must follow both U.S. policy and host-country (local) environmental regulations, which can be stricter.

Documentation and IT asset management turn ad-hoc fixes into a managed service. Asset tags and an inventory/CMDB track every device by serial number, owner, location, and lifecycle status — essential for accountability and especially for controlled or classified hardware. Core documentation includes network topology diagrams (physical and logical maps of the network), a knowledge base and runbooks (step-by-step operational procedures), and a ticketing system that records every request and incident for traceability and metrics. Governing policies include Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), an Acceptable Use Policy (AUP) defining permitted use of government systems, security and password policies, and Non-Disclosure Agreements (NDAs) protecting sensitive information.

Change management is the formal discipline that prevents well-intentioned changes from causing outages. A standard change request moves through defined stages: a documented request describing the change and its purpose; a defined scope (what is and is not affected); a risk analysis weighing impact and likelihood of problems; an implementation plan with steps, owners, and timing; and — critically — a rollback (back-out) plan to restore the prior state if the change fails. The request is reviewed and approved, typically by a Change Advisory Board (CAB) for significant changes, and scheduled in a maintenance window. After implementation, end-user acceptance confirms the change works as intended, and everything is documented (updated diagrams, CMDB, knowledge base). Changes are commonly typed as standard (pre-approved, low-risk, routine), normal (full CAB review), and emergency (expedited approval for urgent fixes, documented retroactively).
keyPoints: ESD can damage components at as little as 10–30V, far below the ~3,000V a human can feel — so latent/catastrophic damage occurs with no warning.
Server rooms target ~40–60% relative humidity: too dry promotes ESD, too humid promotes condensation/corrosion.
An anti-static wrist strap uses a ~1 MΩ resistor to bleed charge safely; NEVER wear it on high-voltage CRTs or power supplies.
Anti-static defenses: grounded wrist strap, ESD mat, Faraday anti-static bags, equipment grounding, and self-grounding by touching bare grounded metal.
A fuse breaks a circuit once and must be replaced; a circuit breaker does the same job but is resettable; use a Class C extinguisher on electrical fires.
Lift with the legs not the back, keep loads close, avoid twisting, and get help/a cart for heavy gear (rule of thumb >~50 lb).
Power-protection spectrum: surge suppressor (spikes only, rated in joules) → UPS (battery ride-through) → generator (sustained outages).
UPS topologies: standby (transfer delay), line-interactive (adds AVR — common for servers), online/double-conversion (zero transfer, isolates load).
The SDS (formerly MSDS) is the authoritative source for a material's hazards, handling, PPE, and disposal.
Li-ion batteries, toner/ink cartridges, and CRTs (lead) are regulated e-waste — recycle via certified channels and obey host-country law at an overseas post.
Asset management uses asset tags + an inventory/CMDB tracking serial, owner, location, and lifecycle for every device.
Change management requires request, scope, risk analysis, plan, approval (often a CAB), rollback plan, end-user acceptance, and documentation.

flashcards.front: At roughly what voltage can ESD damage a sensitive component, and why is that dangerous?
flashcards.back: As little as 10–30 volts — far below the ~3,000V a person can feel — so damage (including delayed 'latent' failures) happens with no warning.

flashcards.front: What relative humidity range is targeted in a server room, and why?
flashcards.back: About 40–60%. Too dry increases static/ESD; too humid causes condensation and corrosion.

flashcards.front: Why does an anti-static wrist strap contain a ~1 MΩ resistor?
flashcards.back: To bleed static charge to ground slowly and safely, and to protect the wearer from shock.

flashcards.front: When must you NEVER wear an anti-static wrist strap?
flashcards.back: When working on high-voltage equipment such as a CRT monitor or a power supply, because their stored charge could pass through you.

flashcards.front: How do you self-ground when no wrist strap is available?
flashcards.back: Maintain continuous contact with a bare metal, grounded part of the chassis to equalize electrical potential before handling components.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a fuse and a circuit breaker?
flashcards.back: A fuse melts to break the circuit once and must be replaced; a circuit breaker trips on overcurrent but can be reset.

flashcards.front: Which fire extinguisher class is used on energized electrical fires?
flashcards.back: Class C.

flashcards.front: Give the proper technique for lifting heavy equipment.
flashcards.back: Lift with your legs (bend the knees, keep your back straight), hold the load close, avoid twisting, and get help or a cart for heavy items (e.g., >~50 lb).

flashcards.front: What does a surge suppressor protect against, and what doesn't it do?
flashcards.back: It clamps transient voltage spikes (rated in joules); it does NOT protect against sags, brownouts, or outages.

flashcards.front: How does a line-interactive UPS differ from a standby UPS?
flashcards.back: A line-interactive UPS adds an automatic voltage regulator (AVR) to correct sags/surges without using the battery; a standby UPS only switches to battery on failure with a brief transfer time.

flashcards.front: What is the key advantage of an online (double-conversion) UPS?
flashcards.back: It continuously powers the load from the inverter, giving zero transfer time and full isolation from line problems (at higher cost).

flashcards.front: What role does a generator play relative to a UPS?
flashcards.back: The generator provides sustained long-run power during extended outages; the UPS bridges the gap until the generator starts and stabilizes.

flashcards.front: What is an SDS (formerly MSDS) and what does it contain?
flashcards.back: A Safety Data Sheet — the authoritative reference for a material's hazards, safe handling, storage, required PPE, first aid, and disposal.

flashcards.front: How should lithium-ion batteries and CRT monitors be disposed of?
flashcards.back: As regulated e-waste through certified recycling — never in regular trash/landfill; Li-ion can ignite if damaged and CRTs contain lead.

flashcards.front: What is a CMDB / IT asset inventory used for?
flashcards.back: Tracking every device by asset tag, serial number, owner, location, and lifecycle status for accountability and control.

flashcards.front: What is a rollback (back-out) plan in change management?
flashcards.back: A predefined procedure to restore the system to its previous working state if the change fails.

flashcards.front: What is a CAB in change management?
flashcards.back: A Change Advisory Board — the group that reviews and approves significant change requests, weighing risk, scope, and scheduling.

flashcards.front: List the core stages of a change request.
flashcards.back: Request, defined scope, risk analysis, implementation plan, approval (often by a CAB), rollback plan, end-user acceptance, and documentation.

title: Backup, Continuity, Privacy & Professionalism
summary: How an embassy IT shop protects data through layered backups and disaster recovery, responds to incidents with proper evidence handling, safeguards regulated information, secures remote access and scripting, and upholds professional conduct at post.
content:
Reliable data protection rests on choosing the right backup methods and combining them sensibly. A full backup copies all selected data every time — simplest and fastest to restore, but slowest to run and largest in storage. An incremental backup copies only data changed since the last backup of any type, which is fast to create and small, but restoration requires the last full plus every incremental in order. A differential backup copies all data changed since the last full backup, so it grows over the week but restores from just the full plus the most recent differential. A synthetic full is constructed by the backup software by merging an existing full with subsequent incrementals — giving you a fresh full image without re-reading all source data. Backup software tracks what has changed using the archive bit or modern change-tracking; understanding the trade-off between backup speed and restore complexity is the core exam point.

The guiding strategy is the 3-2-1 rule: keep at least 3 copies of data, on 2 different media types, with 1 copy off-site. Off-site (including cloud) protects against site-level disasters like fire, flood, or theft, while on-site copies enable fast routine restores. Snapshots capture a point-in-time state of a volume or VM and are excellent for quick rollback, but a snapshot on the same storage is NOT a backup — it shares the fate of the underlying system. The single most-neglected control is backup testing/verification: a backup is worthless until a test restore proves the data is intact and recoverable, so periodic restore drills and integrity checks are mandatory. Frequency and retention are set by the business: how often you back up bounds your potential data loss, and a retention policy (often following a grandfather-father-son rotation) defines how long copies are kept to meet operational and legal requirements.

• Disaster recovery (DR) translates these tools into recovery targets. The Recovery Point Objective (RPO) is the maximum tolerable amount of data loss, measured in time — it dictates backup frequency (a 1-hour RPO means backing up at least hourly). The Recovery Time Objective (RTO) is how quickly a system must be restored after an outage. In U.S. federal and embassy contexts, this falls under Continuity of Operations (COOP) planning, which ensures essential mission functions continue during and after a disruption, including alternate sites and communications.
• Incident response follows a disciplined sequence so problems are handled and evidence is preserved. The basic flow is: identify/detect the incident; report it through the proper channels (for an embassy, the designated ISSO/security officer — never social media or unofficial channels); preserve data and maintain chain of custody (the documented, unbroken record of who handled evidence, when, and why); contain, eradicate, and recover; and document everything, including a lessons-learned review. A key rule: do not power off or 'clean up' a suspected compromised machine before guidance, because that can destroy volatile evidence in memory.

Data handling and privacy are governed by classification and law. You must recognize regulated data categories: PII (Personally Identifiable Information — data that identifies a person, e.g., SSN, passport number), PHI (Protected Health Information under HIPAA), and CUI (Controlled Unclassified Information — sensitive but unclassified federal information requiring safeguarding). Data is handled according to its classification level, and embassy environments add national-security classifications (e.g., Confidential/Secret/Top Secret) processed only on appropriately accredited systems. Protection measures include encryption in transit (TLS) and at rest (e.g., BitLocker/FileVault, FIPS 140-validated modules in federal use), a data retention schedule, and secure data destruction at end of life — shredding, degaussing magnetic media, or cryptographic erase, with certificates of destruction for accountability. The governing principle is least privilege and need-to-know.

Remote-access and support tools are powerful and therefore high-risk. RDP (Remote Desktop Protocol, TCP 3389) gives full graphical control of a Windows host and is a frequent ransomware entry point — it should never be exposed directly to the internet and must sit behind a VPN with MFA. SSH (TCP 22) provides encrypted remote command-line access and secure file transfer, and is the secure replacement for legacy tools. A VPN creates an encrypted tunnel so remote sessions cross untrusted networks safely. RMM (Remote Monitoring and Management) and screen-sharing/remote-support tools let technicians administer many endpoints, but a compromised RMM platform is catastrophic, so they require strong authentication and tight access control. The classic cautionary contrast is Telnet (TCP 23), which transmits everything — including credentials — in clear text and must never be used on a network; SSH replaces it entirely.

• Basic scripting concepts are part of operational tooling. Common types and uses: .bat / .cmd (Windows batch — simple legacy automation), .ps1 (PowerShell — the modern Windows administration and automation standard), .sh (shell scripts on Linux/macOS), .py (Python — cross-platform, general-purpose automation), .vbs (VBScript — legacy Windows), and .js (JavaScript — web and Node automation). Scripts frequently rely on environment variables (system-level values such as PATH or %USERPROFILE% / $HOME) rather than hard-coding paths or secrets. The risks of running scripts are significant: a malicious or careless script runs with the user's privileges and can delete data, change configuration, or install malware, so you must never run an untrusted script, review code before execution, run with least privilege, and respect execution policies (e.g., PowerShell's signed-script requirement).

Professional conduct and communication are weighted heavily in this role because an embassy IT officer is a customer-facing representative. Practice active listening (let the user fully explain, restate the issue to confirm understanding) and avoid jargon or acronyms with non-technical users — match your language to the audience. Set and meet expectations: give a realistic timeframe, communicate status, and follow up; under-promising and over-delivering builds trust. With difficult or frustrated customers, stay calm and professional, do not argue or take it personally, empathize, and focus on resolving the problem — escalate when appropriate rather than overstepping. Maintain confidentiality of everything you see on user systems, be punctual and dependable, and dress and behave appropriately. Finally, cultural sensitivity is essential at post: an embassy is staffed by Americans and locally employed staff from the host nation, so be aware of cultural norms, language differences, and customs, and treat all colleagues and customers with respect.
keyPoints: Full = everything (easy restore, slow/large); incremental = changes since last backup (fast/small, complex restore); differential = changes since last full (middle ground).
A synthetic full merges an existing full with later incrementals to create a fresh full without re-reading all source data.
3-2-1 rule: at least 3 copies, on 2 media types, with 1 copy off-site (cloud counts as off-site).
A snapshot on the same storage is NOT a backup; a backup is unproven until a test restore verifies recoverability.
RPO = maximum tolerable data loss (drives backup frequency); RTO = maximum tolerable downtime (drives restore speed); federal continuity = COOP.
Incident response: identify → report through proper channels (ISSO/security officer) → preserve data & chain of custody → contain/eradicate/recover → document.
Do not power off or alter a suspected compromised machine before guidance — it destroys volatile (memory) evidence.
Know regulated data: PII (identifies a person), PHI (health, HIPAA), CUI (controlled unclassified federal info); handle per classification and need-to-know.
Secure destruction methods: shredding, degaussing (magnetic media), and cryptographic erase, with certificates of destruction.
RDP (3389) and Telnet (23) are dangerous exposed; Telnet is clear-text and must be replaced by SSH (22); expose RDP only behind VPN+MFA.
Script types: .bat/.cmd, .ps1 (PowerShell), .sh, .py, .vbs, .js; use environment variables and never run untrusted scripts (they run with your privileges).
Professionalism: active listening, avoid jargon, set/meet expectations, handle difficult customers calmly, keep confidentiality, be punctual, show cultural sensitivity at post.

flashcards.front: Compare full, incremental, and differential backups.
flashcards.back: Full copies all data (easy restore, slow/large). Incremental copies changes since the last backup of any type (fast/small, but restore needs the full plus every incremental). Differential copies changes since the last full (restore needs only the full plus the latest differential).

flashcards.front: What is a synthetic full backup?
flashcards.back: A full backup image assembled by the backup software by merging an existing full with subsequent incrementals, without re-reading all the original source data.

flashcards.front: State the 3-2-1 backup rule.
flashcards.back: Keep at least 3 copies of data, on 2 different media types, with at least 1 copy stored off-site.

flashcards.front: Why is a snapshot not a substitute for a backup?
flashcards.back: A snapshot on the same storage shares the fate of the underlying system; if that storage fails, the snapshot is lost too.

flashcards.front: Why must backups be tested?
flashcards.back: A backup is worthless until a test restore proves the data is intact and actually recoverable; restore drills catch corruption and gaps.

flashcards.front: Define RPO and how it relates to backup frequency.
flashcards.back: Recovery Point Objective is the maximum tolerable amount of data loss (in time); it dictates how often you must back up (a 1-hour RPO requires at least hourly backups).

flashcards.front: Define RTO.
flashcards.back: Recovery Time Objective — the maximum tolerable time to restore a system/service after an outage.

flashcards.front: What does COOP mean in the federal/embassy context?
flashcards.back: Continuity of Operations — planning that ensures essential mission functions continue during and after a disruption, including alternate sites and communications.

flashcards.front: What are the basic steps of incident response?
flashcards.back: Identify/detect, report through proper channels, preserve data and chain of custody, contain/eradicate/recover, and document (including lessons learned).

flashcards.front: Why shouldn't you power off a suspected compromised machine immediately?
flashcards.back: Powering off or 'cleaning' it destroys volatile evidence in memory (running processes, network connections, keys) before it can be preserved.

flashcards.front: What is chain of custody?
flashcards.back: The documented, unbroken record of who handled evidence, when, and why — essential to its integrity and admissibility.

flashcards.front: Define PII, PHI, and CUI.
flashcards.back: PII = Personally Identifiable Information (identifies a person). PHI = Protected Health Information under HIPAA. CUI = Controlled Unclassified Information — sensitive federal info requiring safeguarding.

flashcards.front: Name three secure data-destruction methods.
flashcards.back: Physical shredding, degaussing of magnetic media, and cryptographic erase — ideally documented with a certificate of destruction.

flashcards.front: Why is Telnet considered insecure, and what replaces it?
flashcards.back: Telnet (TCP 23) sends all data, including credentials, in clear text; SSH (TCP 22) provides an encrypted replacement.

flashcards.front: What ports do RDP and SSH use, and how should RDP be exposed?
flashcards.back: RDP uses TCP 3389 and SSH uses TCP 22; RDP should never be directly internet-facing — place it behind a VPN with MFA.

flashcards.front: Match script extensions to their use: .ps1, .sh, .py, .bat.
flashcards.back: .ps1 = PowerShell (modern Windows admin); .sh = Linux/macOS shell scripts; .py = Python (cross-platform automation); .bat/.cmd = legacy Windows batch.

flashcards.front: What is the main risk of running an untrusted script?
flashcards.back: It runs with the user's privileges and can delete data, alter configuration, or install malware — so review code first and run with least privilege.

flashcards.front: What is an environment variable, with an example?
flashcards.back: A named system-level value scripts read instead of hard-coding (e.g., PATH, %USERPROFILE% on Windows or $HOME on Linux).

flashcards.front: How should you handle an angry or difficult customer?
flashcards.back: Stay calm and professional, listen and empathize, don't argue or take it personally, focus on resolving the issue, and escalate when appropriate.

flashcards.front: Why is cultural sensitivity important for an embassy IT officer?
flashcards.back: Posts include American and locally employed host-nation staff; awareness of cultural norms, language, and customs is essential to professional, respectful service.
